title: CCA-F Exam Guide
description: Official exam guide flashcards — all 5 domains, 6 scenarios

flashcards.question: You are building a customer support agent using the Agent SDK. Your agent calls a tool to look up order status, but instead of stopping, it keeps looping and calling the same tool repeatedly. What is the most likely cause?
options: The tool is returning an error that the agent interprets as needing retry
You are not appending the tool result back to the messages array after execution
The system prompt is too long and confuses the model
The tool_choice is set to "any" instead of "auto"
flashcards.explanation: В агентском цикле (agentic loop) после каждого вызова инструмента необходимо добавить результат (tool_result) обратно в массив сообщений. Если этого не делать, модель не видит ответ и продолжает запрашивать тот же инструмент. D тоже может вызвать проблемы, но наиболее вероятная причина бесконечного цикла

flashcards.question: Your agentic loop receives a response with `stop_reason: "end_turn"`. What should your code do next?
options: Parse the response for tool_use blocks and execute them
Return the final response to the user --- the agent has finished its task
Retry the request because end_turn indicates an error
Append an empty tool_result and continue the loop
flashcards.explanation: `stop_reason: "end_turn"` означает, что модель завершила свою работу и не запрашивает никаких инструментов. Это сигнал для выхода из цикла и возврата ответа пользователю. `stop_reason: "tool_use"`

flashcards.question: You are designing a multi-agent research system with a coordinator that delegates to specialized subagents. The coordinator needs to synthesize results from a "web search" subagent and a "database query" subagent. Which pattern best describes this architecture?
options: Sequential pipeline with prompt chaining
Hub-and-spoke with a coordinator and isolated subagents
Peer-to-peer mesh where agents communicate directly
Single agent with multiple tool calls in one turn
flashcards.explanation: Паттерн hub-and-spoke (звезда) предполагает центральный координатор, который делегирует задачи специализированным подагентам с изолированным контекстом. Координатор декомпозирует задачу, распределяет подзадачи и синтезирует результаты. Это отличается от последовательного пайплайна (A) и одного агента с несколькими инструментами (D).

flashcards.question: In a hub-and-spoke multi-agent system, why is it important that each subagent has an isolated context?
options: To reduce API costs by sending fewer tokens
To prevent subagents from accessing each other's tools and leaking information across tasks
To allow subagents to run on different LLM providers
To enable parallel execution on multiple CPU cores
flashcards.explanation: Изоляция контекста подагентов гарантирует, что каждый работает только со своей задачей и инструментами. Это предотвращает утечку информации между задачами и повышает безопасность. Снижение стоимости (A)

flashcards.question: You are configuring the Agent SDK to spawn a subagent for code review. Which configuration is required for the subagent to be able to spawn its own child agents?
options: Set max_depth to a value greater than 1
Include "Task" in the allowedTools array of the AgentDefinition
Set fork_session to true in the parent agent
Add a system prompt instructing the subagent to use Task tool
flashcards.explanation: Для того чтобы подагент мог порождать собственные дочерние агенты, инструмент "Task" должен быть включён в массив allowedTools его AgentDefinition. Без этого подагент не сможет вызвать Task tool, даже если в промпте будет соответствующая инструкция.

flashcards.question: A developer is building a compliance agent that must ALWAYS redact PII from tool outputs before they enter the conversation context. What is the most reliable approach?
options: Add detailed instructions in the system prompt about PII redaction
Use a PostToolUse hook that programmatically scans and redacts PII from every tool result
Train a custom model fine-tuned on PII redaction
Ask the user to manually review each tool output
flashcards.explanation: PostToolUse хуки обеспечивают программное (programmatic) применение правил после каждого вызова инструмента. В отличие от промпт-инструкций (A), хуки гарантируют 100% выполнение

flashcards.question: Your team needs to normalize all currency values returned by various MCP tools to USD before the agent processes them. Where should this logic be implemented?
options: In each individual MCP tool's implementation
In the system prompt as a formatting instruction
In a PostToolUse hook that intercepts and transforms tool results
In the agent's final response formatting step
flashcards.explanation: PostToolUse хуки идеально подходят для нормализации данных

flashcards.question: You are building a document processing pipeline where Step 1 extracts text, Step 2 classifies the document, and Step 3 routes it. Each step always follows the previous one. Which orchestration pattern is most appropriate?
options: Dynamic adaptive decomposition with a coordinator agent
Fixed sequential pipeline using prompt chaining
Hub-and-spoke with parallel subagents
Single monolithic prompt with all steps
flashcards.explanation: Когда шаги всегда выполняются последовательно и предсказуемо, фиксированный последовательный пайплайн (prompt chaining)

flashcards.question: A developer's agent session was interrupted mid-task after making several file changes. The developer wants to continue from where it left off. What should they do?
options: Start a new session and re-describe all changes made so far
Use `--resume` to continue the existing session with its full context
Use `fork_session` to create a new branch of the conversation
Manually undo all changes and start fresh
flashcards.explanation: Флаг `--resume` позволяет продолжить прерванную сессию с полным контекстом

flashcards.question: When should you use `fork_session` instead of `--resume` in Claude Code?
options: When you want to retry a failed command
When you want to explore an alternative approach while preserving the original session state
When the context window is full
When switching between different Git branches
flashcards.explanation: `fork_session` создаёт ответвление сессии для параллельного исследования альтернативного подхода, сохраняя оригинальную сессию нетронутой. Это полезно когда нужно попробовать другой путь без потери текущего прогресса. `--resume`

flashcards.question: You are building a customer support agent with 18 different tools including email lookup, order status, refund processing, FAQ search, and more. The agent frequently calls the wrong tool. What is the most effective fix?
options: Add more detailed system prompts explaining each tool
Reduce to 4-5 scoped tools relevant to the current task context
Set tool_choice to "any" to force tool usage
Increase the model's temperature for more creative tool selection
flashcards.explanation: Исследования показывают, что 18 инструментов значительно снижают надёжность выбора по сравнению с 4-5. Решение

flashcards.question: Your MCP tool returns an error when the user provides an invalid email format. How should the error be structured for the agent to handle it properly?
options: Return a generic string "Error occurred"
Return with isError: true and metadata including errorCategory: "validation" and isRetryable: false
Throw an unhandled exception
Return an empty result with HTTP status 400
flashcards.explanation: Структурированные ошибки с `isError: true`, категорией ошибки и флагом `isRetryable` позволяют агенту принять правильное решение. Validation-ошибка не повторяемая (isRetryable: false)

flashcards.question: A tool call fails because of a temporary network timeout. The error metadata shows `errorCategory: "transient"` and `isRetryable: true`. What should the agent do?
options: Immediately return an error to the user
Ask the user to fix their network connection
Retry the tool call, possibly with backoff
Switch to a different tool that doesn't require network
flashcards.explanation: Transient-ошибки (временные сбои сети, таймауты) помечаются `isRetryable: true`, что сигнализирует агенту о необходимости повторной попытки. В отличие от validation или permission ошибок, transient-ошибки часто разрешаются сами по себе.

flashcards.question: You write a tool description: "This tool does stuff with databases." Why is this problematic?
options: It's too short for the API to process
Vague descriptions prevent the model from correctly selecting the tool and understanding its boundaries
Database tools require authentication headers in descriptions
Tool descriptions must be written in JSON format
flashcards.explanation: Описания инструментов

flashcards.question: You have two MCP tools: "search_documents" and "find_files". Agents frequently confuse them. What is the best solution?
options: Remove one of the tools entirely
Rewrite descriptions with clear boundary explanations --- search_documents for content search, find_files for filename/path patterns
Force tool_choice to always use search_documents
Merge them into a single tool
flashcards.explanation: Пересекающиеся описания (overlapping descriptions)

flashcards.question: Your team configures MCP servers for a project. Where should project-specific MCP server configurations go so all team members share them?
options: In each developer's ~/.claude.json
In the project's .mcp.json file committed to the repository
In environment variables on each machine
In the system prompt of every conversation
flashcards.explanation: `.mcp.json` в репозитории проекта

flashcards.question: In Claude Code, you need to find all TypeScript files matching a specific naming pattern. Which built-in tool should you use?
options: Grep
Glob
Read
Bash with find command
flashcards.explanation: Glob

flashcards.question: You are setting up Claude Code for your team's monorepo. The root CLAUDE.md contains general coding standards, and each package directory has its own CLAUDE.md with package-specific rules. How does Claude Code resolve these?
options: Only the root CLAUDE.md is loaded
Only the nearest CLAUDE.md to the current file is loaded
All levels are loaded hierarchically --- user, project, and directory-level files are merged
The developer must manually specify which CLAUDE.md to use
flashcards.explanation: Claude Code поддерживает иерархию CLAUDE.md: user-level (~/.claude/), project-level (корень проекта) и directory-level (поддиректории). Все уровни загружаются и объединяются, позволяя задать общие правила на верхнем уровне и специфичные

flashcards.question: Your team wants conditional rules that only apply when editing Python files in the `backend/` directory. How should you configure this in Claude Code?
options: Add Python-specific instructions to the root CLAUDE.md
Create a file in `.claude/rules/` with YAML frontmatter containing a `paths` field matching `backend/**/*.py`
Create a separate CLAUDE.md in every Python subdirectory
Use environment variables to toggle rules
flashcards.explanation: Файлы в `.claude/rules/` с YAML frontmatter и полем `paths` обеспечивают условную загрузку правил

flashcards.question: You want to create a reusable "/deploy" command for your team that runs a specific deployment workflow. Where should this be defined?
options: In the system prompt of each conversation
In `.claude/commands/` directory as a markdown file with optional frontmatter
In the project's package.json scripts
As a bash alias in each developer's shell profile
flashcards.explanation: Custom commands определяются в `.claude/commands/` как markdown-файлы. Они могут включать frontmatter с `context: fork`, `allowed-tools` и `argument-hint` для настройки поведения. Это стандартный механизм Claude Code для повторно используемых команд.

flashcards.question: A developer needs to refactor a complex authentication system spanning 15 files. Should they use plan mode or direct execution?
options: Direct execution --- just let Claude make all changes at once
Plan mode --- to first understand the codebase, create a plan, then execute incrementally
Neither --- manual refactoring is safer for complex changes
Direct execution with a very detailed prompt
flashcards.explanation: Для сложных задач, затрагивающих множество файлов, plan mode позволяет сначала исследовать кодовую базу, составить план изменений и выполнять их пошагово. Direct execution подходит для простых изменений. Explore subagent помогает понять архитектуру перед изменениями.

flashcards.question: You want Claude Code to generate a REST API handler that matches your team's patterns. What prompting technique will produce the best results?
options: Ask Claude to "write a good REST API handler"
Provide concrete I/O examples showing the expected request/response format and reference an existing handler file
Write a detailed specification in natural language only
Let Claude auto-detect patterns from the codebase without guidance
flashcards.explanation: Конкретные примеры ввода-вывода (I/O examples)

flashcards.question: Your CI pipeline needs to run Claude Code for automated code review on every pull request. Which flags are essential?
options: `--interactive` and `--verbose`
`-p` flag for non-interactive mode and `--output-format json` for structured output
`--resume` to continue from the last review
`--debug` and `--trace` for logging
flashcards.explanation: Флаг `-p` запускает Claude Code в неинтерактивном режиме (CI-friendly), а `--output-format json` с `--json-schema` обеспечивает структурированный вывод, который легко парсить в пайплайне. Контекст сессии изолирован, что важно для независимых ревью.

flashcards.question: You are writing a system prompt for a classification agent. Instead of "Try to classify emails correctly," what should you write?
options: "Please do your best to classify emails"
"Classify each email into exactly one of: billing, technical_support, general_inquiry. If confidence < 0.7, return 'needs_human_review'"
"Be very careful when classifying emails"
"Use AI to smartly classify all emails"
flashcards.explanation: Эксплицитные критерии (explicit criteria)

flashcards.question: Your structured data extraction agent sometimes formats phone numbers as "(555) 123-4567" and sometimes as "5551234567". How should you fix this inconsistency?
options: Add a rule: "Format phone numbers consistently"
Provide few-shot examples showing the exact acceptable format, plus an example of a common wrong format labeled as unacceptable
Post-process all outputs with regex
Use a smaller model that is more consistent
flashcards.explanation: Few-shot примеры

flashcards.question: You are building a structured data extraction system. You need the agent to always return a JSON object with specific fields. What is the most reliable approach?
options: Ask the model to "return JSON" in the system prompt
Use tool_use with a JSON schema that defines the exact output structure
Parse the model's text output with regex
Use XML tags to wrap the output
flashcards.explanation: `tool_use` с JSON-схемой

flashcards.question: What is the difference between setting `tool_choice: "auto"` vs `tool_choice: "any"` in the API?
options: "auto" is faster, "any" is more accurate
"auto" lets the model decide whether to use a tool, "any" forces the model to call at least one tool
"auto" selects the best tool, "any" calls all tools
They are identical in behavior
flashcards.explanation: `tool_choice: "auto"`

flashcards.question: Your extraction pipeline validates JSON output against a schema, but the output sometimes has semantic errors (e.g., "age: -5"). The JSON is syntactically valid. What approach should you add?
options: Use a stricter JSON schema with more type constraints
Add semantic validation that checks business rules, then use retry-with-error-feedback including a detected_pattern field
Increase the model temperature to avoid repetitive errors
Switch to XML output format
flashcards.explanation: Схемная валидация (syntax) не ловит семантические ошибки вроде отрицательного возраста. Нужна дополнительная семантическая валидация бизнес-правил. Retry-with-error-feedback с полем `detected_pattern` позволяет модели понять и исправить систематические ошибки.

flashcards.question: Your company needs to process 10,000 customer reviews for sentiment analysis overnight. Real-time responses are not required. Which API feature offers the best cost-efficiency?
options: Standard API calls with caching
Message Batches API with 50% cost savings and a 24-hour processing window
Streaming API for faster throughput
Extended thinking for deeper analysis
flashcards.explanation: Message Batches API предлагает 50% экономию при пакетной обработке в окне 24 часа. Идеально для оффлайн-задач без требований к реальному времени. Ограничение: Batches API не поддерживает tool calling. Каждый запрос в батче идентифицируется через custom_id.

flashcards.question: A developer sets up a self-review where the same Claude instance that wrote the code also reviews it. What is the main limitation of this approach?
options: The model cannot read its own code
The model has inherent blind spots reviewing its own output and tends to confirm its original reasoning
Self-review is too expensive
The API doesn't support multiple turns
flashcards.explanation: Самопроверка (self-review) имеет фундаментальное ограничение: модель склонна подтверждать своё собственное рассуждение. Для надёжной проверки нужны независимые экземпляры (independent review instances), multi-pass стратегия: сначала per-file, затем cross-file ревью.

flashcards.question: You are optimizing API costs for a customer support agent that sends the same long system prompt with every request. Which feature should you use?
options: Shorten the system prompt to save tokens
Use prompt caching with cache_control breakpoints on the system prompt (minimum 1024 tokens)
Move the system prompt into a tool description
Send the system prompt only on the first request
flashcards.explanation: Prompt caching позволяет кешировать часто повторяющиеся блоки (системный промпт, примеры) с минимумом 1024 токена. `cache_control` breakpoints указывают, что кешировать. Порядок обработки важен

flashcards.question: Your agent uses extended thinking for complex reasoning tasks. During streaming, you receive a thinking block. What must you verify before trusting it?
options: That the thinking block contains valid JSON
That the thinking block's signature is valid --- it proves the thinking was genuinely produced by Claude
That the thinking block is shorter than 1000 tokens
That the thinking block mentions all tools used
flashcards.explanation: Блоки extended thinking имеют криптографическую подпись (signature), которая подтверждает, что содержимое действительно сгенерировано Claude. Это защита от подмены. Redacted thinking

flashcards.question: Your agent's conversation has grown to 180K tokens out of a 200K context window. Performance is degrading. What should you do?
options: Start a completely new session, losing all context
Use summarization to condense older messages, or use `/compact` to let Claude summarize the conversation
Increase the context window to 500K
Remove the system prompt to free up tokens
flashcards.explanation: При приближении к лимиту контекстного окна (200K токенов) стратегия суммаризации сжимает старые сообщения, сохраняя ключевую информацию. `/compact` в Claude Code автоматически суммирует разговор. Системный промпт (D) удалять нельзя

flashcards.question: Your customer support agent encounters a request to cancel a $50,000 enterprise contract. The agent normally handles routine cancellations automatically. What pattern should be implemented?
options: Process the cancellation automatically like any other
Use confidence-based routing with escalation --- high-value cases trigger human-in-the-loop with structured escalation data
Refuse the request entirely
Ask the customer to call back later
flashcards.explanation: Паттерн эскалации (escalation pattern) с маршрутизацией по уверенности (confidence-based routing) критичен для высокорисковых операций. Агент должен передать дело человеку (human-in-the-loop) со структурированными данными: контекст, причина эскалации, рекомендация. Это баланс между автоматизацией и безопасностью.

flashcards.question: You are designing a multi-agent research system (Scenario 3). The coordinator receives a research question and must decide how to split work between a "web search" subagent and a "data analysis" subagent. What determines if the coordinator uses fixed or dynamic task decomposition?
options: The number of subagents available
Whether the subtasks are predictable in advance (fixed) or depend on intermediate results (dynamic adaptive)
The model size being used
The number of tokens in the research question
flashcards.explanation: Если подзадачи заранее предсказуемы (всегда "найди данные" → "проанализируй")

flashcards.question: In your CI/CD pipeline (Scenario 5), Claude Code runs a code review with `-p "Review this PR for security issues" --output-format json --json-schema schema.json`. Why is `--json-schema` important?
options: It makes the review faster
It ensures the output matches a predefined structure that your CI tooling can reliably parse and act on
It reduces API costs
It enables extended thinking
flashcards.explanation: `--json-schema` гарантирует, что выходные данные ревью соответствуют заданной структуре (severity, location, description и т.д.), которую CI-инструменты могут надёжно парсить. Без схемы вывод может быть в произвольном формате, что ломает автоматизацию.

flashcards.question: You need to enforce that all API responses go through a prerequisite security check before being sent to the user. The check must never be skippable. What approach ensures this?
options: Prompt-based guidance: "Always run security checks before responding"
Programmatic enforcement using hooks with prerequisite gates that block response until the check passes
Training the model to always remember security checks
Adding security check as the last tool in the toolset
flashcards.explanation: Программное enforcement через хуки с prerequisite gates (обязательными условиями) гарантирует выполнение проверки

flashcards.question: You are configuring a Skills directory for your team's deployment workflows. What file must be present in the skill directory to make it discoverable?
options: README.md
SKILL.md
index.ts
config.yaml
flashcards.explanation: `SKILL.md`

flashcards.question: Your agent is building a customer support system (Scenario 1) that uses MCP tools for CRM access. The CRM tool sometimes returns "rate limit exceeded" errors. How should the error be categorized?
options: errorCategory: "validation", isRetryable: false
errorCategory: "transient", isRetryable: true
errorCategory: "permission", isRetryable: false
errorCategory: "business", isRetryable: false
flashcards.explanation: Rate limit

flashcards.question: You are using the @import syntax in CLAUDE.md. What does `@import ./standards/coding-rules.md` do?
options: Downloads a remote file from the standards repository
Includes the contents of the referenced file as if they were part of this CLAUDE.md
Creates a symbolic link to the file
Validates that the referenced file exists without loading it
flashcards.explanation: Синтаксис `@import` в CLAUDE.md включает содержимое указанного файла как часть текущего CLAUDE.md. Это позволяет модульно организовать инструкции

flashcards.question: In a developer productivity tools scenario (Scenario 4), your agent needs to edit a specific function in a large file. The Edit tool requires the old_string to be unique. The function signature appears twice in the file. What should you do?
options: Use Write tool to rewrite the entire file
Include more surrounding context in old_string to make it unique within the file
Use Bash with sed to make the edit
Ask the user to manually rename one of the functions first
flashcards.explanation: Edit tool требует уникальности `old_string` в файле. Если строка повторяется, нужно включить больше окружающего контекста (соседние строки), чтобы фрагмент стал уникальным. Переписывать весь файл (A)

flashcards.question: Your batch processing job uses the Message Batches API. Each request needs a unique identifier for tracking. What field serves this purpose?
options: request_id
custom_id
batch_index
message_uuid
flashcards.explanation: `custom_id`

flashcards.question: You are implementing prompt caching for an agent with a 3000-token system prompt and 200-token few-shot examples. What is the minimum you must cache?
options: Any block of at least 512 tokens
Any block of at least 1024 tokens --- the system prompt qualifies but the few-shot examples alone do not
Any block of at least 100 tokens
Caching has no minimum token requirement
flashcards.explanation: Prompt caching требует минимум 1024 токена для кешируемого блока. Системный промпт в 3000 токенов подходит. Few-shot примеры в 200 токенов

flashcards.question: A code generation scenario (Scenario 2) requires Claude Code to understand an unfamiliar codebase before making changes. Which approach follows best practices?
options: Immediately start writing code based on the user's description
Use plan mode with the Explore subagent to understand architecture, then use test-driven iteration with the interview pattern to clarify requirements
Ask the user to explain every file in the codebase
Run all tests first and infer the architecture from test names
flashcards.explanation: Best practice: сначала plan mode + Explore subagent для понимания архитектуры, затем test-driven iteration (пишем тесты → код → проверяем) и interview pattern (уточняющие вопросы к пользователю). Это предотвращает ошибки от непонимания кодовой базы.

flashcards.question: Your multi-agent system has a coordinator that spawns subagents for different tasks. A subagent encounters a situation it cannot handle (e.g., user's request is ambiguous). What should happen?
options: The subagent should make its best guess and proceed
The subagent should return structured escalation data to the coordinator, which decides whether to ask the user for clarification
The subagent should directly ask the user for clarification
The subagent should terminate silently
flashcards.explanation: В hub-and-spoke архитектуре подагенты не общаются с пользователем напрямую. При неопределённости подагент возвращает структурированные данные эскалации координатору: что неясно, какие варианты, рекомендация. Координатор решает

flashcards.question: You notice that your agent with extended thinking enabled sometimes returns "redacted" thinking blocks. What does this mean?
options: The thinking exceeded the budget and was truncated
The thinking contained content that was filtered for safety reasons, but the response is still valid
The model encountered an error during reasoning
The API key doesn't have permission for extended thinking
flashcards.explanation: Redacted thinking

flashcards.question: You are configuring a custom command in `.claude/commands/review.md` that should run in a forked session and only use specific tools. Which frontmatter fields do you need?
options: `mode: fork` and `tools: [Grep, Read]`
`context: fork` and `allowed-tools: [Grep, Read, Edit]`
`session: new` and `restrict-tools: true`
`fork: true` and `toolset: limited`
flashcards.explanation: Frontmatter custom commands использует `context: fork` для запуска в отдельной (forked) сессии и `allowed-tools` для ограничения доступных инструментов. Также доступен `argument-hint` для подсказки аргументов. Это стандартный формат определения commands в Claude Code.

flashcards.question: In a structured data extraction scenario (Scenario 6), your agent extracts invoice data using tool_use with a JSON schema. The schema requires a "total" field as a number, but the model returns `"total": "1,234.56"` (a string). What type of error is this?
options: A semantic error --- the value is meaningful but wrong type
A syntax error --- the JSON is malformed
A schema validation error --- the field type doesn't match the schema definition
A transient error that will resolve on retry
flashcards.explanation: Это ошибка схемной валидации (schema validation)

flashcards.question: Your team uses MCP resources to provide reference documentation to agents. An MCP server exposes a "product catalog" resource. How should agents access this?
options: Through a tool call to a dedicated "get_catalog" tool
MCP resources serve as content catalogs that are loaded into context, not invoked as tools
Through a direct HTTP request to the MCP server
By reading the .mcp.json configuration file
flashcards.explanation: MCP resources

flashcards.question: You are implementing a structured handoff protocol between two agents in a pipeline. Agent A completes data collection and must pass results to Agent B for analysis. What is the key requirement for reliable handoffs?
options: Both agents must share the same context window
Use a structured handoff protocol with explicit data format, validation of completeness, and clear task boundaries
Agent A must directly inject messages into Agent B's context
Use a shared database that both agents query
flashcards.explanation: Структурированный протокол передачи (structured handoff protocol) требует: явный формат данных, валидацию полноты (все ли нужные поля заполнены), и чёткие границы задач. Это обеспечивает надёжную передачу между агентами без потери информации. Общий контекст (A) нарушает изоляцию.

title: Claude Code in Action
description: CLI AI assistant for development workflows

flashcards.question: What is the fundamental mechanism that allows language models inside coding assistants to read files, write code, and run commands?
options: Direct file system access built into the model weights
Tool use - the model responds in a formatted way, and the assistant executes the requested action
A separate indexing service that pre-processes the entire codebase
The model downloads and executes shell scripts autonomously
flashcards.explanation: Языковые модели сами по себе умеют только принимать текст и возвращать текст. Инструменты (tool use)

flashcards.question: According to the course, what is the core strength of Claude Code as a coding assistant?
options: Its ability to generate code faster than other models
Claude's particularly strong tool use capabilities
Built-in support for all programming languages
Automatic code indexing of the entire codebase
flashcards.explanation: В курсе явно сказано: «Claude's strong tool use is the absolute core strength of Claude Code.» Благодаря этому Claude может эффективно решать сложные задачи, комбинируя инструменты. A

flashcards.question: What does the `/init` command do when you first run Claude Code in a project?
options: Installs all project dependencies
Creates a CLAUDE.md file with a summary of the codebase architecture
Initializes a Git repository
Sets up MCP server connections
flashcards.explanation: Команда `/init` заставляет Claude глубоко изучить весь проект и создать файл CLAUDE.md с описанием архитектуры, ключевых файлов и команд. Этот файл включается в каждый запрос к Claude. A

flashcards.question: Which levels of CLAUDE.md files does Claude Code support?
options: Only a single project-level file
Project level, local level, and machine (global) level
Project level and user level
Repository level and branch level
flashcards.explanation: Claude Code поддерживает три уровня: project-level (коммитится в Git, общий для команды), local-level (не коммитится, личные инструкции), и machine/global-level (применяется ко всем проектам на машине). A неверен

flashcards.question: In Claude Code, what does the `#` shortcut do during a conversation?
options: Starts a code comment
Enters memory mode to edit CLAUDE.md files
Opens the file search interface
Activates extended thinking
flashcards.explanation: Ввод `#` в Claude Code переводит в «memory mode», позволяя интеллектуально добавлять инструкции в один из файлов CLAUDE.md. Это удобный способ запоминать правила и предпочтения без ручного редактирования файла. A

flashcards.question: How can you reference a specific file in a Claude Code conversation to include its contents?
options: Use the `/file` command
Use the `@` character followed by the filename
Use the `#include` directive
Copy and paste the file contents manually
flashcards.explanation: Символ `@` позволяет упомянуть файл, и его содержимое автоматически включается в запрос к Claude. Это же можно использовать в CLAUDE.md для автоматического включения важных файлов. A

flashcards.question: What is the purpose of the `/compact` command in Claude Code?
options: Minifies the generated code
Summarizes the current conversation history to maintain learned knowledge while reducing context
Compresses files in the project directory
Switches to a more compact display mode
flashcards.explanation: Команда `/compact` суммирует все сообщения в текущем разговоре, сохраняя знания Claude о задаче, но уменьшая объём контекста. Полезно при переходе между подзадачами в рамках одной темы. A

flashcards.question: When should you use the `/clear` command vs `/compact` in Claude Code?
options: `/clear` for any task switch, `/compact` never
`/clear` when starting an unrelated task, `/compact` when switching to a related subtask
They are interchangeable
`/compact` for long conversations, `/clear` to free memory
flashcards.explanation: `/clear` полностью сбрасывает историю разговора

flashcards.question: What are the two ways to boost Claude Code's intelligence for harder tasks?
options: Increasing temperature and adding more context
Plan mode (Shift-Tab) and extended thinking (trigger phrases like "ultrathink")
Using a larger model and increasing max_tokens
Adding MCP servers and custom commands
flashcards.explanation: Plan mode (активируется через Shift-Tab) заставляет Claude больше исследовать проект и составлять детальный план. Extended thinking (фразы: think, megathink, ultrathink) даёт больше токенов на рассуждение. Они могут использоваться вместе. A

flashcards.question: When is Plan mode most useful in Claude Code?
options: When debugging a specific line of code
When a task requires wide understanding of the codebase and multiple steps
When generating a single function
When running tests
flashcards.explanation: Plan mode полезен для задач, требующих широкого понимания кодовой базы и нескольких шагов

flashcards.question: What are hooks in Claude Code?
options: Git hooks that run on commit
Commands that run before or after Claude attempts to use a tool
Plugins that add new language support
Event listeners for file system changes
flashcards.explanation: Hooks

flashcards.question: In a Claude Code pre-tool use hook, what does an exit code of 2 signal?
options: The hook executed successfully
The tool call should be blocked, and stderr output is sent as feedback to Claude
The hook encountered a fatal error
Claude should retry the tool call
flashcards.explanation: Exit code 0 означает «всё ок, разрешить вызов инструмента». Exit code 2 блокирует вызов и отправляет stderr-логи как фидбек Claude. Это позволяет, например, запретить чтение .env файлов и объяснить Claude причину. A

flashcards.question: Which tools should a pre-tool use hook watch for if the goal is to prevent Claude from accessing a sensitive file's contents?
options: Only the `read` tool
The `read` and `grep` tools
The `read`, `write`, and `edit` tools
All tools
flashcards.explanation: Для предотвращения доступа к содержимому файла нужно следить за `read` (прямое чтение) и `grep` (поиск по содержимому файла). `write` и `edit` изменяют файлы, но не читают чувствительные данные. Мониторить все инструменты избыточно.

flashcards.question: How do you add an MCP server to Claude Code?
options: Edit the configuration file manually
Run `claude mcp add <name> <command>` at the terminal
Install it through the VS Code extension marketplace
Use the `/install-mcp` command inside Claude Code
flashcards.explanation: Команда `claude mcp add` в терминале (не внутри Claude Code) позволяет добавить MCP-сервер, указав имя и команду для запуска. Например, `claude mcp add playwright ...`. A

flashcards.question: How can you allow Claude Code to use an MCP server's tools without permission prompts each time?
options: Run Claude Code as administrator
Add `mcp__<server_name>` to the allow array in settings.local.json
Set `--auto-approve` flag when starting Claude Code
Disable all security checks in the configuration
flashcards.explanation: В файле `.claude/settings.local.json` в массив `allow` нужно добавить строку `mcp__<имя_сервера>` (с двумя подчёркиваниями). Это разрешает использование всех инструментов этого MCP-сервера без запроса разрешения. A

flashcards.question: What is the Claude Code SDK primarily useful for?
options: Building standalone AI applications
Using Claude Code programmatically as part of larger pipelines, scripts, or hooks
Creating mobile applications with Claude
Training custom Claude models
flashcards.explanation: SDK позволяет использовать Claude Code программно

flashcards.question: What is a key limitation of the Claude Code SDK by default?
options: It cannot access the internet
It only has read abilities --- no write, edit, or create permissions
It can only run Python code
It has a 5-minute timeout
flashcards.explanation: По умолчанию SDK имеет только права на чтение (read, grep, list directories). Для записи нужно явно добавить разрешения через параметр `allowTools` в коде или через файл настроек. A

flashcards.question: How do you create a custom slash command in Claude Code?
options: Register it in the package.json file
Create a markdown file in `.claude/commands/` directory with the command name as filename
Use the `/register` command
Add it to the CLAUDE.md file
flashcards.explanation: Создаёте файл `.claude/commands/<имя>.md`

flashcards.question: What does the `/install-github-app` command set up in Claude Code?
options: A GitHub Copilot integration
Two GitHub Actions: one for mentioning support (@Claude) and one for automated PR reviews
A webhook for deployment notifications
A GitHub bot for issue tracking only
flashcards.explanation: Команда устанавливает приложение Claude Code на GitHub и создаёт PR с двумя GitHub Actions: одно для обработки упоминаний @Claude в issues/PR, другое для автоматического ревью pull requests. Оба можно кастомизировать. A

flashcards.question: When running Claude Code inside a GitHub Action with MCP servers, what special requirement exists for permissions?
options: No special requirements --- all permissions are inherited
Each individual tool from each MCP server must be explicitly listed in the permissions
Only the MCP server name needs to be listed
Permissions are automatically granted based on the repository settings
flashcards.explanation: При работе в GitHub Actions нужно явно перечислить каждый инструмент каждого MCP-сервера. Нет короткого способа (wildcard) разрешить все инструменты сразу, в отличие от локального использования. A

flashcards.question: A post-tool use hook runs a TypeScript type checker after Claude edits a .ts file and finds a new error. What happens next?
options: The hook silently logs the error and continues
The error feedback is sent back to Claude, which then attempts to fix the introduced type error
Claude Code automatically reverts the change
The user is prompted to fix the error manually
flashcards.explanation: Post-tool use hook может отправить обратную связь Claude через stderr. Claude получает информацию об ошибке типизации, понимает, что внёс ошибку, и пытается исправить вызовы в других файлах. Это показано в курсе как практический пример полезного hook. A

flashcards.question: What keyboard shortcut is used to paste a screenshot into Claude Code?
options: Cmd+V (on macOS)
Ctrl+V (even on macOS)
Shift+V
Alt+V
flashcards.explanation: Для вставки скриншота используется Ctrl+V (не Cmd+V, как обычно на macOS). Ctrl+V специально предназначен для вставки изображений. Это позволяет показать Claude, что именно вы видите на экране. A

flashcards.question: What is the recommended approach when Claude Code repeatedly makes the same mistake?
options: Restart Claude Code each time
Press Escape to stop Claude, then use # (memory mode) to add a correction to CLAUDE.md
Switch to a different model
Report the bug to Anthropic
flashcards.explanation: Комбинация Escape (остановить Claude) + # (memory mode) для добавления правила в CLAUDE.md

flashcards.question: How does pressing Escape twice work in Claude Code?
options: It exits Claude Code
It shows conversation history allowing you to rewind to a previous message
It cancels the current file edit
It clears the terminal
flashcards.explanation: Двойное нажатие Escape показывает историю отправленных сообщений, позволяя «перемотать» разговор назад к предыдущей точке. Это полезно для удаления лишнего контекста (например, длительной отладки) и продолжения с чистого состояния. A

flashcards.question: In the hook that detects duplicate queries in a codebase, what technology is used to launch a separate analysis?
options: A child process running grep
The Claude Code TypeScript SDK to programmatically launch a second Claude Code instance
A database query to check for duplicates
A static analysis tool like ESLint
flashcards.explanation: В примере с query review hook используется Claude Code TypeScript SDK для программного запуска отдельной копии Claude Code. Эта копия анализирует изменение, находит существующие запросы и возвращает рекомендации основному экземпляру. A

knowledgeBase.content:
I've got my code editor open inside that small project, and I'm going to start up the development server with a NPM run dev. And when I run that, I'm going to be able to navigate to a local host 3000 inside my browser and see the application running. So here it is right here. We are going to use Claude to do a little bit of work on this project. But first, there's something really critical I want you to understand around using Claude. Specifically, I want you to walk away from this course with a strong understanding of context management. You see, inside of your typical project, there might be dozens or hundreds of files, each with a tremendous amount of information. Whenever we ask Claude a question or give it a task, there is some ideal amount of information that Claude needs. Just enough to help to understand how to answer your question or complete your task.

As soon as we start adding in additional information that's not relevant, Claude's effectiveness will start to decrease. So it is really important for us to help guide Claude towards relevant files or documentation inside of our project. Claude code can certainly work without any handholding, but you'll get the best results if you provide just a little bit of guidance. So for the remainder of this video, I'm going to give you a bunch of different tips on how to give Claude the best context possible. To get started, inside my editor, I've opened up my terminal, and I'm going to start Claude Code up by running the Claude command. Whenever you run Claude Code in a project for the first time, I highly recommend running the slash init command. This gets Claude to take a deep look at your entire code base. It'll figure out the purpose of the project, the general architecture, relevant commands, critical files, and so on.

After this search, it'll summarize its findings and place them into a file called Claude.md. When Claude tries to create this file, it will ask for permission. You can either hit Enter to accept, or if you don't want to have to grant permission to every file write request, you can also press Shift Tab, which will allow Claude Code to freely write files in your project. I would encourage you to open up the Claude MD file that was generated and take a look at its contents. As I mentioned, the contents of this file are included in every request we make off the Claude. This file really has two different purposes. First, it helps Claude better understand your codebase so it can find relevant code more quickly. And second, it serves as a location where you can give Claude some general guidance. Just so you know, there are multiple Claude MD files that Claude Code will make use of.

There is a project level, a local level, and a machine level. The project level is what we just generated by running the slash init command. We are generally going to commit this file to source control, like Git. We're going to share this file with other engineers and just have some project specific directions that we want to hand off to Claude. Optionally, we can also create a Claude local MD file. This file is not going to be committed and you're generally not going to share with any other engineers. Inside this file, you might put in some personal instructions that you want Claude to follow just for you. Finally, you can have a global Claude MD file on your machine. This file will contain instructions or be applied to all projects that you run locally. Now, I keep on mentioning giving Claude special or custom instructions. So let me show you an example of that.

Let's imagine that Claude is using comments way too often in the code that it writes. We can address this by updating our Claude MD file. We can either manually modify the file or a little bit of a shortcut is inside of Claude Code we could put in a pound sign. This puts us in memory mode. This allows us to edit one of our Claude MD files intelligently. So we can put in a request like, don't write comments so often. I'll then specify that I want to add this instruction to the project Claude MD file, and Claude is then going to merge this instruction into that file intelligently. If I then open the file up and do a search, I'll see that in fact, yes, it did add in that new instruction. Now that we've created our Claude MD file, I want to give you a better understanding of how to pull in specific context into a conversation.

Let's imagine that we want to better understand how the authentication system in this project works. We could just ask Claude to tell us about it, in which case, we would search over our codebase and find files relevant to the authentication system. That would definitely work, but it would just take some amount of time. Alternatively, if we already know some files that are relevant for the authentication system, we could mention them using the ATT character. When we mention a file, it will be automatically included inside of our request off to Claude. This is an excellent technique fo

Just a moment ago, I made some pretty big claims by saying that Claude was an expert at making use of tools. And that Claude code was easily extensible. Naturally, you might be a little skeptical, so I'd like to give you a few quick demonstrations. On this table are the default tools that are available in Claude code. It has all the abilities you would expect, like reading files, writing files, running commands, and so on. I'm going to show you a couple of tasks completed using Claude Code. And in each case, it will use this set of tools in rather intelligent ways. And in at least one task, I'll even give Claude an additional new set of tools to make use of. Not only will this process give you a good idea of what Claude code can do right out of the box, but hopefully you will also see how easily you can extend Claude code with more functionality.

Here's my first task for Claude code. I'm going to ask it to find and optimize performance issues in the chalk library. In case you're not familiar with it, chalk is a JavaScript package. Here's the documentation for it. Now this is a very small library that has one very simple purpose. All it does is print out text in nicely formatted colors, so like exactly as you see in this example screenshot right here. So you can give the text colors or backgrounds and formatting, all that kind of stuff. Now, this might sound like a really simple and silly package, but here's the thing. It turns out this is actually the fifth most downloaded package in the entire JavaScript ecosystem. Last week in particular, it had 429 million downloads. So this package is used far and wide to put it simply. If I could find any way to optimize anything inside this package, well, it would probably be worth the effort.

So I'm going to ask Claude to run the benchmarks, identify the worst performing cases, use some profiling tools to figure out why those cases are running so slowly, and then fix them. We'll then see that Claude is going to use a wide variety of different tools to intelligently tackle this problem. It'll form up a 2DList to track its progress, execute commands to run the benchmarks, write a file to better zoom in on one particular case, use a CPU profiler to understand why that case is running so slowly, and then implement some improvements. By the end, we'll get a 3.9 times improvement in throughput in one particular operation around this library. Here's another example of how well Claude can string together different tool calls to complete a rather complex task. I'm going to give it a data set inside the CSV file. All the data inside of here contains information about different users of a video streaming platform.

And I'm going to ask it to just do a general analysis, maybe identify some causes of churn on the platform. And I want all this analysis to be done inside of a Jupyter notebook. Here's my data set. I'm then going to ask Claude to run the analysis and let's see how it does. This is a great example of where effective tool use is really important. You see, it's not really enough that Claude just writes code into a notebook. Claude can also execute code in different cells and view the results of those executions. That means that Claude can take some initial look at the data in the notebook and then customize each successive cell to hone in on some particular details. Next up, I'd like to show you an example of a task where I extend Claude Code's capabilities by giving it access to a new set of tools.

I built a small app that will generate UI components based upon some description entered on the left side of the screen. The generated component is then displayed on the right side. Now, the app can generate good looking components quite easily, but the chat interface on the left and the header at the top are not looking so nice. So I'm going to use Claude Code to improve the styling. If I just asked it to fix the styling and the chat interface in the header, it would likely do a fine job. But remember, my goal here is to show you how easy it is to add additional functionality to Claude Code. So along with this styling task, I'm going to also give Claude Code access to a new set of tools provided by something called the Playwright MCP server, which I'll tell you more about later on.

These tools allow Claude to directly open and control a browser. So here's what that process looks like in action. I'm going to ask Claude to improve the styling of my app and make use of a browser to do so. It'll then open a browser on the right-hand side of the screen. Navigate to my app, it'll take a screenshot to view the current styling, and then update the styling. We could even ask Claude to take another screenshot of the page when it was complete and iterate on the design a couple of times to really get a nice design that really pops. And before long, we've got something that actually looks pretty reasonable. There's one last set of demonstrations that I like to give you. Remember wha

In this video, I'd like to show you a couple of different techniques for controlling and directing the flow of conversation. Here's a basic example right away. I'm going to ask Claude to write tests for some functions written into a authentication file. Claude quickly comes up with a plan for authoring several different tests. However, I know that testing this file is a little tough, and I'd like Claude to only test one thing at a time. To interrupt Claude, I can press escape. This will stop Claude in its tracks, allowing me to suggest a different path. Combining Escape, along with memories, is a really powerful way to fix errors that Claude makes repeatedly. Here's an example. I'm going to ask Claude to write tests for the same file again. This time around, it will attempt to read a test configuration file that doesn't actually exist. Now, this is an error that I've seen Claude make before on this project.

So to stop this mistake from being repeated, I'll very quickly hit Escape. I'll then use the pound shortcut to add in a memory about the correct name of this test config file. And now, I probably won't have to see this error again. Some of these conversation control shortcuts seem like they're just for convenience. But use correctly, they can really improve Claude's ability to work effectively and stay on task. So let me show you a more practical example. Inside the auth.ts file, there are four different functions. And I would like to get Claude to write tests for each of them one at a time, first starting on a function called create session. Claude will definitely attempt to write the tests, but as it is running them, it runs into an error and spends a little bit of time debugging it. It turns out there was a package that I forgot to install.

Eventually the tests are completed and working, and it's time to start working on the next set of tests. But here's the thing. In my conversation history, there is now a lot of back and forth around that broken package. Now this is a bunch of context that is not at all relevant to writing the next set of tests. Ideally, we would be able to jump back in time and go back to the previous message we sent and just update it to say, write test for a git session. Now, the benefit here is that we maintain the context where Claude already took a look at the contents of the auth.ts file. And it already knows what we're talking about when we refer to git session. And because we dumped all those extra messages that were just about debugging, we're not going to have as much distraction going on here.

So again, Claude can really just stay focused in on task. To go back in the conversation history, hit Escape twice. This will show you all the different messages that you have set, so you can rewind back to a previous point in time and skip over some intermediate conversation. Claude is now going to start working on the next set of tests. This time around, Claude stays super focused, but unfortunately, it runs into a number of issues. It eventually resolves them and gets the test to pass. Now at this point, Claude has been working by itself for several minutes and has a really good idea of how to write tests for this file. At the same time, once again, we have a bunch of context in this conversation history. When it is time to write tests for the next function, I want to use a command called compact.

The compact command will take all the messages in the current conversation and summarize them. Compact is really useful when Claude has learned a lot about the current task and you want to maintain that knowledge as it goes into the next task. The last context-related command to be aware of is the Clear command. Clear will dump the entire conversation history, allowing you to start off from scratch. Clear is most useful anytime you're about to start on a completely different task unrelated to the current one. I recommend using these shortcuts quite a bit, particularly when you are changing between tasks or anytime you are having a long-running conversation with Claude. In the remainder of this course, we'll use them several times to make sure that Claude stays on task and focused.

When you run Claude Code, you can enter in a forward slash and see a bunch of commands that are built into Claude Code by default. In addition to these default commands, you can easily add your own custom commands as well. Custom commands are useful for automating repetitive tasks that you find yourself running frequently. Let me show you an example of how to make one. Inside of my project directory, I'm going to find the .Claude folder. Inside there, I'll make a new directory called commands. And then inside that, I'll make a new file called audit.md. The name of the file that we create, in this case audit, is going to be the name of the command we eventually run. The goal of this command is going to be to audit all the different dependencies that have been installed into this project, update them if there are any vulnerabilities, and then run tests to make sure that nothing actually broke.

Once you have created the command file, you'll then restart Claude Code. Don't forget to restart it. When you reopen Claude Code, put in slash audit. This will then display the command that you just created. You can then run this, and in this case, it will do exactly what we ask Claude to do. It'll run a command, see if there are any vulnerable packages, fix them if necessary, and then run tests. Commands can also receive arguments. Let me show you an example. I'm going to make another command called write test. Whenever I run this command, I want to have some tests created for a very particular file inside my project. Inside of the command text, I'm going to put in a placeholder of dollar sign arguments. Whenever I run the command, if I pass in a path to a file, that path will be inserted at dollar sign arguments.

So now I can restart Claude Code again and then execute the write test command. Now, to be clear, the arguments we pass in don't have to be a file path. It can be any string we want to pass in. So I might casually ask for tests for a file in some particular folder, giving Claude just a little bit of direction on where to look.

To get a better idea of how hooks work, we're going to take a look at a new sample project. Attach this lecture as a file called Query.zip. I'd encourage you to download this project and open your code editor inside of it. Once you've got your editor open, at your terminal, Run, NPM, Run, Setup. This is going to install a couple of dependencies and get a couple of hooks ready for use. To better understand hooks, we're going to make our own inside of this project. So here's what I want our hook to do. Inside the root directory of our project is a file called .env. This file contains some sensitive information. And out of an abundance of caution, I want to completely prevent Claude from ever reading this file directly. Let me show you a couple of diagrams to help you understand how we're going to put this hook together.

Step one is to decide on whether we need a pre-tool use or a post-tool use hook. In this scenario, we want to prevent Claude from ever reading a particular file. If we make a post-tool use block, then we will have executed our hook or ran our command after Claude already read the file. So in this case, we definitely need a pre-tool use hook to make sure that we can prevent the read operation from occurring. The next thing we need to do is decide exactly which kind of tool calls we want to watch for. I've got a list of all the different current tool names on the right hand side of this diagram. Now, memorizing all the different tool names that are included inside of Claude Code can be really challenging, especially since you can add your own custom tools through the use of MCP servers.

So let me show you a little trick you can use here. If I go back over and open up Claude Code, I can directly ask Claude for a bullet point list of all the different tool names that it has access to right now. Out of all these different tools, there are two that can be used to very easily read the contents of a file. First, there's the retool, and then it's easy to miss, but this one can actually read the contents of a file as well, the GREP tool. GREP can search the contents of a file. So we really want to watch for tool calls for the retool and the GREP tool. Next up, we need to write out a command that is going to receive some information about the tool called that Claude wants to make. Here's how that part works. We're going to write out a command, Claude is going to automatically execute it.

And then on standard in to that process, Claude is going to feed in some tool called data as JSON. I've got an example of some tool called data on the top right hand side. So it's going to be a big JSON object that has some information about the tool name and the input to that tool. In this case, the tool name is read, so Claude is trying to call the read tool, and it might be trying to read specifically a file path pointing to that .e and v file. And again, that's the file that we want to prevent a read operation for. So then inside of our program or our command, we need to receive this information through standard in, parse that JSON, and then read the tool name, the tool input arguments, and so on, and decide what we want to do with this tool call.

Then onto step four. In step four, after our command receives that proposed tool called data, we're then going to exit. And our exit code is going to provide a signal back to Claude Code. An exit code of zero means everything is OK, and we want to allow this tool call to occur. An exit code of two, however, is a sign-to-Claude Code that we want to block this tool call. And that specifically only applies for the pre-tool use hooks. Because remember, only in a pre-tool use hook can we actually block a tool call. If we exit with a code of two, then any standard air logs that we generated inside of our command during that time will also be sent as feedback to Claude. So we can both deny the tool call and give Claude a reason at the same time as well. So that's the entire process.

And I know once again, there's a lot of stuff going on here. So let's go through this entire process of wiring everything up needed for this hook inside of our project to understand how all these steps come together.

Claude code has an official GitHub integration that allows Claude code to run inside of a GitHub action. You can set up this integration by running slash install GitHub app. This will walk you through a couple of steps. First, you'll need to install the Claude code app on GitHub. Next, you'll need to add in an API key. And then after this, a pull request will be automatically generated. This pull request adds in two different GitHub actions. The first action adds in mentioning support. So from an issue or a pull request, you can mention Claude with @Claude and give Claude some kind of task to run. The second action adds in support for reviewing pull requests. So whenever you create a pull request, Claude code will automatically run and review the proposed changes. Both of these actions can be customized, and you can also add in additional actions to trigger based on other types of events.

Let me show you how you can customize the mentioning feature. First, we just merged in those two action config files to our repository on GitHub. So I need to pull those changes down to my local machine. Then inside of the newly created GitHub workflows directory, I'll see these two action config files. One adds in support for the pull request review, and the other adds in support for handling mentions. Now, here's how I want to customize the mention functionality. Whenever I mention Claude inside of an issue or a pull request, I wanted to be able to run the project and use the Playwright MCP server to access the app inside of a web browser, all inside of a GitHub action. To make this work, I'll first add in a step before Claude code runs in this workflow. I'll run the setup command and then start the development server up.

Then I'm going to update the Claude code configuration. I'll add in some custom instructions. These are passed directly to Claude and they allow us to provide some additional directions or context. In this case, I'll tell Claude that the development server is already running and that I can use the Playwright MCP server to access the app in the browser if needed. Then I will add in some configuration to set up the Playwright MCP server itself. There is one other thing to be aware of here. When you're running Claude Code inside of an action, we have to specifically list out all the permissions that we want to grant Claude Code. And there's one tricky aspect to this. If you're using an MCP server, you have to individually list out each tool from each MCP server that you want to allow. There is no shortcut for permissions like we saw previously.

Unfortunately, the Playwright MCP server has many different tools, so they each need to be listed out. Once I've finished with this configuration update, I'll be sure to commit these changes and push them. Now it's time to test out this updated workflow. I'm going to give Claude a little task. In our actual app, see these two buttons up here. Right now they work fine. I can toggle between the preview and the code panels without issue. But I'm going to pretend as though they weren't working as intended. I'm going to take a screenshot with that button right there. I'm going to make an issue. I'm going to paste in the screenshots. And I'm going to mention Claude with @Claude and ask it to verify that the two buttons are working as intended. I'll then create the issue and wait. Now, it is going to take a minute or two for the action to actually start up and for Claude's response.

Remember, as we just saw in the action, we are now setting up the entire app and starting it running before Claude Code even starts to run at all. But eventually, Claude will respond. It will very often create a checklist of steps to accomplish the given task. In this case, it is going to attempt to visit the app, manually test out the button, and fix any issues that it finds. Claude will notice that the buttons actually are working just fine, and so it's going to terminate early with the message documenting its findings. Now, this is just a small example of how you can use Claude Code's GitHub integration. I recommend you spend some time to think about how you can custom tailor it for your own particular project.

Claude directory, I'm going to open up the settings.local.json file. Remember, inside of here, we have a list of pre-tool use hooks and post-tool use hooks. As we discussed a moment ago, we want to make a pre-tool use hook so that we can prevent Claude from ever reading the contents of that particular file. I already added in a little configuration section right here for us, just to save us a little bit of typing. All we need to do is fill in the matcher and the command. First is the matcher.

So the matcher is going to be the tools that we want to watch for. In our case, as we discussed, we want to watch for calls to the read and the grep tools.
